name: GE Aerospace

profile.description: GE Aerospace designs and produces commercial and defense aircraft engines, integrated engine components, electric power, and aircraft systems. The company operates through Commercial Engines & Services (LEAP, GE90, GEnx, CF6, CFM56) and Defense & Propulsion Technologies segments, with a $190B+ backlog serving airlines, lessors, and defense customers worldwide.

innovation.label: STRONG
innovation.analysis: Track Record (3/3): GE Aerospace completed the historic separation from the GE conglomerate in 2024 and emerged as a focused aerospace pure-play delivering accelerating results — Q1 2026 adjusted revenue surged 29% to $11.6B with orders up 87% to $23.0B and engine unit deliveries up 43% [1]. The company continues to ramp LEAP engine production toward 2,000 annual units. AI Deployment (1/2): CEO Culp confirmed AI deployment for predictive maintenance, engine health monitoring, and manufacturing workflow optimization, but no specific revenue figures or quantified savings have been disclosed — AI is described as augmenting worker performance rather than a revenue line [2]. Market Share (2/2): CFM International (GE-Safran JV) commands approximately 60% of the narrowbody engine market, with LEAP powering both the Boeing 737 MAX and Airbus A320neo — the two most popular aircraft families in history [1]. Pipeline (2/2): LEAP production ramp toward 2,000 annual units, $1B+ investment in MRO capacity expansion, next-generation engine technologies targeting 20%+ fuel efficiency improvement, T901 military engine program, and GE9X for Boeing 777X — all with specific milestones and funded development [1]. R&D Intensity (1/1): GE Aerospace invests $2.3B+ annually in R&D spanning next-gen propulsion, sustainable aviation fuel compatibility, advanced materials, and additive manufacturing — well above aerospace industry averages [3].

inspiration.label: STRONG
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): GE Aerospace's purpose of 'inventing the future of flight' is specific, thoughtful, and deeply differentiated — connecting to a century of aviation innovation from early jet engines to the LEAP platform [2]. This purpose survived the painful GE breakup and emerged strengthened as the company's sharpened identity under Culp's leadership. The commitment to sustainable aviation and net-zero by 2050 extends the cause into the next century. Community Impact (1/2): GE Aerospace employs 57,000 people globally with multiple Employee Resource Groups (Pride Alliance, Women's Network, Green Team, veterans groups) and invests in STEM education, though specific charitable dollar amounts were not surfaced [3]. Employee Retention (2/2): Glassdoor 4.1/5 rating based on 3,394+ reviews with culture, benefits, and career development cited as top strengths [2]. The FLIGHT DECK lean operating model explicitly prioritizes 'Respect for People' and continuous improvement, creating above-average employee experience in the industrial sector. Insider Buying (1/2): Culp has significant equity exposure through the company, and the $15B share repurchase authorization with 25% dividend increase signals leadership conviction — though this is programmatic capital return rather than personal open-market buying [1].

infrastructure.label: STRONG
infrastructure.analysis: GE Aerospace's growth metrics are exceptional — Q1 2026 revenue surged 29% to $11.6B, with FY2025 revenue of approximately $48.3B and 5-year revenue CAGR of ~17.5% [1]. ROE of 45.4% dramatically exceeds the 15% target, and profit margin of 17.9% is near industrial excellence. Free cash flow of $1.7B in Q1 (+14%) with full-year guidance of $8.0-8.4B and >100% FCF conversion demonstrates world-class cash generation [1]. The primary drags are valuation and leverage: Forward P/E of 47.6x (up from 32x at prior screen) reflects the aerospace super-cycle premium but is well above the 20x target, and PEG of 3.15 exceeds the 1.0x target, scoring zero on that metric. D/E of 117% is elevated with $21.3B debt against $10.6B cash, though the massive cash generation provides comfortable coverage [4]. Liquidity floor easily cleared at approximately $2.1B daily (5.6M shares × $369) [4].

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (8/9): GE Aerospace's core business — designing, manufacturing, certifying, and servicing jet engines — is a physical-world, deeply regulated operation that AI structurally cannot displace [1]. Aircraft engines require decades of metallurgical expertise, FAA/EASA certification, and safety-critical manufacturing that no AI system can replicate. The aerospace MRO business benefits from fleet aging — as global aircraft supply remains constrained and fleets age, airlines spend more on maintenance, creating a captive service revenue stream backed by $170B in services backlog [1]. Defense engine programs (T901, F414) are long-cycle government contracts immune to AI substitution. Moreover, AI is a net positive for aerospace — AI data centers increase electricity demand, driving air travel for business and supply chain logistics. The $190B+ backlog provides multi-year revenue visibility that AI disruption cannot erode [1]. AI Opportunity (1/1): GE Aerospace deploys AI for predictive engine health monitoring, manufacturing process optimization, MRO workflow improvements, and digital twin technology — CEO Culp confirmed active AI deployment across operations [2].

infinite_game.summary: Under Larry Culp, GE Aerospace exemplifies infinite game thinking. The painful but necessary dismantling of the GE conglomerate to focus on the enduring aviation business was one of the most significant acts of existential flexibility in corporate history. The FLIGHT DECK lean operating model, rooted in Respect for People and continuous improvement, creates organizational durability. Culp's patient, multi-year transformation from a bloated conglomerate to a focused aerospace pure-play is Sinek's playbook executed at scale — destroying what existed to protect what endures.

just_cause.analysis: GE Aerospace's purpose of 'inventing the future of flight' has endured through 100+ years of aviation history — from early jet engines to today's LEAP platform powering the world's most popular aircraft [1]. This Just Cause survived GE's near-death experience under Immelt and Flannery, and was sharpened by Culp's laser focus post-separation. The commitment to sustainable aviation (net-zero by 2050, SAF research) extends the cause into future decades. The purpose genuinely shapes strategy — the entire corporate restructuring was executed to protect and advance this cause. However, it has not yet been proven through a full economic downturn as a standalone entity. (2/3 points)

trusting_teams.analysis: Culp's FLIGHT DECK operating model explicitly prioritizes psychological safety with the hierarchy 'safety, quality, delivery, and cost — always in that order' [2]. The lean foundational behaviors of 'respect for people, continuous improvement, and customer-driven' create structural trust throughout the organization. Glassdoor 4.1/5 rating with culture and career development cited as top pros validates the internal experience. Culp's leadership philosophy emphasizes that 'speaking up about risk, quality, or concern is not just allowed but expected' — this is Sinek's Circle of Safety operationalized in a 57,000-person industrial organization [2]. (2/2 points)

worthy_rivals.analysis: GE Aerospace operates in duopoly with Rolls-Royce (widebody engines) and through CFM International with Safran (narrowbody) — the joint venture structure itself demonstrates learning from a worthy rival rather than seeking to destroy it [1]. The partnership with Safran, now spanning decades, is fundamentally about combining complementary strengths rather than competing to win. The company's competitive approach focuses on technology advancement and operational excellence. (1/1 point)

existential_flexibility.analysis: The breakup of General Electric — one of America's most iconic conglomerates, formerly the most valuable company in the world — into three focused companies is among the most significant acts of existential flexibility in modern corporate history [1]. Culp chose to destroy the GE conglomerate structure that previous CEOs tried desperately to preserve, spinning off GE HealthCare and GE Vernova to create a pure-play aerospace company. This was painful, controversial, and transformative — precisely the kind of pivot Sinek describes as serving the Just Cause over institutional inertia. The focused company has thrived: backlog expanded to $190B+, margins expanding, FCF exceeding $7.7B annually [1]. (2/2 points)

courage_to_lead.analysis: Culp demonstrated extraordinary courage by dismantling the GE conglomerate that every previous CEO fought to preserve, prioritizing long-term aerospace focus over conglomerate earnings power [1]. The $1B+ investment in MRO capacity expansion during supply chain disruption demonstrates willingness to invest when the outcome is uncertain. The FLIGHT DECK lean transformation requires leaders to admit the prior system was broken — a culturally courageous act at a 130-year-old organization. However, the $15B share buyback authorization at premium valuations and the elevated valuation suggest some financial engineering optimism. (1/2 points)

investment_thesis: GE Aerospace is the world's preeminent jet engine company delivering accelerating results in an aerospace super-cycle — Q1 2026 revenue surged 29% to $11.6B, orders exploded 87% to $23.0B, engine unit deliveries jumped 43%, and services revenue grew 39% with shop visit revenue up 35% [1]. The $190B+ backlog provides years of revenue visibility, with commercial services backlog alone at $170B. FY2026 guidance of $7.10-7.40 EPS, $9.85-10.25B operating profit, and $8.0-8.4B FCF (>100% conversion) demonstrates sustained double-digit growth with world-class cash generation [1]. Culp's FLIGHT DECK lean operating model is driving margin expansion and operational discipline across 57,000 employees [2]. The defense segment is inflecting with DPT book-to-bill above 2.0 for two consecutive quarters and 67% order growth in Q1 [1]. At $369 per share, the premium valuation (47.6x forward PE) is underpinned by a structurally advantaged competitive position — CFM International's ~60% narrowbody engine market share, multi-decade fleet maintenance revenue streams, and unmatched aerospace IP [1].
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on extreme valuation and cyclical risk. At 47.6x forward PE and $384B market cap, GE Aerospace is priced for flawless execution of the aerospace super-cycle — any deceleration in commercial aviation demand, supply chain disruption, or global recession would compress the multiple severely [4]. The PEG ratio of 3.15 suggests the stock is significantly overvalued relative to its growth rate. Q1 2026 operating margins compressed 200bps due to loss-making GE9X ramp costs, and continued engine unit growth (+43%) pressures near-term profitability [1]. D/E of 117% with $21.3B debt creates financial obligations, though $10.6B cash and $8B+ annual FCF provide substantial coverage. The Middle East conflict overshadowed the Q1 earnings, creating geopolitical risk for air travel demand [1]. The aerospace cycle is inherently cyclical — a 2008-style recession would defer airline capital expenditure and compress engine orders. Defense business carries reputational and geopolitical risk. The company has yet to face a full economic downturn as a standalone entity [3].
key_catalysts: Services revenue accelerating at 39% growth with $170B commercial services backlog providing multi-year visibility as global fleet ages and airlines extend aircraft lifespans [1]

Defense & Propulsion Technologies inflection — book-to-bill above 2.0 for two consecutive quarters, 67% order growth in Q1, and T901 engine program positioning the segment for sustained mid-to-high single-digit revenue growth [1]
Free cash flow guidance of $8.0-8.4B with >100% conversion funding $15B share repurchase authorization, 25% dividend increase, and continued MRO capacity expansion [1]
key_risks: Extreme premium valuation at 47.6x forward PE and 3.15 PEG leaves no margin of safety — any deceleration in aviation demand or execution stumble would trigger a significant de-rating [4]
GE9X engine ramp driving 200bps margin compression in Q1 2026, with continued OE growth (+43% units) pressuring near-term profitability even as revenue surges [1]

Geopolitical risk from Middle East conflict and broader macro uncertainty — management acknowledged guidance assumes no global recession, elevated Brent crude through Q3, and softer departures growth [1]

backlog.note: Record backlog of $190B+ with Q1 2026 orders of $23.0B (+87% YoY). Commercial services backlog at $170B. DPT book-to-bill above 2.0 for two consecutive quarters. Backlog-to-revenue ratio of ~3.9 quarters provides strong medium-term visibility.

sources: GE Aerospace Q1 2026 Earnings Release — $11.6B adjusted revenue (+29%), $23.0B orders (+87%), $1.7B FCF, $190B+ backlog, April 22, 2026
GE Aerospace CEO Larry Culp — FLIGHT DECK lean model, AI deployment, T. Rowe Price Long View interview, Glassdoor 4.1/5 rating, 2025-2026
GE Aerospace ESG & Sustainability — net-zero 2050 commitment, Green Team ERG, SAF research, sustainability reporting 2026
